title: 5 Claude Cowork Tricks (in 10 min)
summary: A YouTube video tutorial by Tristen O'Brien demonstrates five practical uses of Claude Cowork, an AI tool by Anthropic, for automating business tasks. The video shows how Claude Cowork can reduce a 10-hour daily administrative workload to a streamlined process by analyzing financial spreadsheets, comparing supplier quotes, and generating business reports for small businesses.
writeup: Claude CoWork, Anthropic's agentic workspace product, is demonstrated across five real admin tasks for an 18-location bulk candy vending business, replacing work the owner estimates at roughly 10 hours per day. The through-line is that CoWork differs from Claude Chat by connecting directly to Gmail, Google Drive, and local desktop files, and by spawning multiple parallel agents (up to 10 requested, 8 actually dispatched) to run sub-tasks concurrently. Each task produces a concrete, reviewable artifact rather than a chat reply, and the supplier comparison task surfaces $251 per year in hidden savings the owner did not know existed.
key_points: CoWork's connector architecture links Gmail, Google Drive, and local desktop files in a single session, persisting those connections across every subsequent session so setup is one-time, not per-task.

Spawning multiple agents in parallel is a first-class feature: a prompt requesting 10 agents resulted in 8 running concurrently, with visible progress tasks (pull files, filter 5-month window, calculate revenue per visit, generate visual) completing in minutes rather than the hours manual analysis would require.

The supplier comparison task (SweetTooth vs. Midwest distributors, analyzed product by product against monthly volume) found $251 per year in potential savings and flagged that premium-product pricing still favored the current distributor, producing a decision-ready executive summary rather than raw numbers.

Receipt categorization read 7 receipts from Google Drive, updated a desktop expense spreadsheet, and flagged an anomalous 30% tax charge on an $8 line item, illustrating that the anomaly-detection instruction is taken literally and acts as a lightweight audit pass.

The 18-location profitability analysis over 5 months (November through March) ranked every location by revenue per visit: top performer was an ice cream parlor at $45.17 per visit ($451.65 total across 10 visits), bottom performer at $6.78 per visit, directly surfacing a keep-or-cut decision the owner had deferred for lack of time to run the analytics.

The final task combined three desktop spreadsheets (collections data, expense tracking, supplier pricing) with same-day Gmail context to produce both a reusable monthly P&L spreadsheet with formulas and an interactive KPI dashboard showing revenue by location, expense breakdown, inventory alerts, and action items in a single run.
